texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: K3PO4, K2HPO4, and KH2PO4 as activating agents were added into the aqueous solution of amino acid salts glycine (GLY) to  form activated absorbents (AAb) for CO2 capture. The performances of GLY and AAb for CO2 capture were evaluated using a membrane contactor-regeneration cycled setup. The effects of activating agent concentration and flow rate on overall voluminal mass transfer coefficient, mass transfer flux, and capture efficiency were studied. The phosphates exhibited remarkable activating effects on CO2 capture in the membrane contactor; the activation extents of the phosphates were in the order of PO4 3> HPO4 2> H2PO4 -.  A small amount of activating agent is sufficient to promote the CO2 capture. The changes of hydrodynamics in the membrane contactor can improve mass transfer performance and enhance mass transfer flux to a certain extent; the essential factor for enhancing the mass transfer efficiency in membrane gas absorption is the performance of absorbents used.
texts.text: Abstract: K3PO4, K2HPO4, and KH2PO4 as activating agents were added into the aqueous solution of amino acid salts glycine (GLY) to  form activated absorbents (AAb) for CO2 capture. The performances of GLY and AAb for CO2 capture were evaluated using a membrane contactor-regeneration cycled setup. The effects of activating agent concentration and flow rate on overall voluminal mass transfer coefficient, mass transfer flux, and capture efficiency were studied. The phosphates exhibited remarkable activating effects on CO2 capture in the membrane contactor; the activation extents of the phosphates were in the order of PO4 3> HPO4 2> H2PO4 -.  A small amount of activating agent is sufficient to promote the CO2 capture. The changes of hydrodynamics in the membrane contactor can improve mass transfer performance and enhance mass transfer flux to a certain extent; the essential factor for enhancing the mass transfer efficiency in membrane gas absorption is the performance of absorbents used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is generally considered as one of the main  causes  that  result  in  the  greenhouse  effect  and  global warming.  It  has  turned  to  be  a  worldwide  issue  to  reduce CO2 emission and decrease CO2 concentration in the atmosphere;  the  capture  of  CO 2 from  the  industrial  sources seems to be an important measure for this issue.
texts.text: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is generally considered as one of the main  causes  that  result  in  the  greenhouse  effect  and  global warming.  It  has  turned  to  be  a  worldwide  issue  to  reduce CO2 emission and decrease CO2 concentration in the atmosphere;  the  capture  of  CO 2 from  the  industrial  sources seems to be an important measure for this issue.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The current processes for CO2 separation and capture are based on absorption, adsorption, conversion, cryogenic separation, and membrane techniques [1] . Hereinto, the membrane  contactor  and  gas  absorption  coupling  process, namely  membrane  gas  absorption  (MGA),  has  attracted significant  attention  because  of  its  high  efficiency,  speed, simple  operation,  energy  saving,  pollution-free,  and  low investment [2] . Over the many years of research and development, MGA has shown considerable progress in the recovery and removal of  CO2 [3,4] ; the development of absorbents with high performance for CO2 capture is one of the  major  research  subjects  in  MGA.  Aqueous  amino  acid salts as absorbents for CO2  have aroused researchers' concern  for  a  long  time.  Glycine  (GLY)  was  used  as  a component  of the complex absorbent in the improved Benfield process (20-30% K2CO3 aqueous solution) [5] , which was  widely  used  in  CO2  removal  for  the  synthetic  gas
texts.text: The current processes for CO2 separation and capture are based on absorption, adsorption, conversion, cryogenic separation, and membrane techniques [1] . Hereinto, the membrane  contactor  and  gas  absorption  coupling  process, namely  membrane  gas  absorption  (MGA),  has  attracted significant  attention  because  of  its  high  efficiency,  speed, simple  operation,  energy  saving,  pollution-free,  and  low investment [2] . Over the many years of research and development, MGA has shown considerable progress in the recovery and removal of  CO2 [3,4] ; the development of absorbents with high performance for CO2 capture is one of the  major  research  subjects  in  MGA.  Aqueous  amino  acid salts as absorbents for CO2  have aroused researchers' concern  for  a  long  time.  Glycine  (GLY)  was  used  as  a component  of the complex absorbent in the improved Benfield process (20-30% K2CO3 aqueous solution) [5] , which was  widely  used  in  CO2  removal  for  the  synthetic  gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: purification  in  80-90s  of  the  last  century.  Moreover,  the aqueous  amino  acid  salts  are  also  used  for  the  selective absorption  of  H2S  from  H2S/CO2 [5] in  the  Alkacid  and Alkacid DIK processes of BASF and Exxon. Environment & Energy  Center,  TNO  the  Netherlands,  developed  a  process called CORAL, which applied the aqueous amino acid salts to  MGA.  The  aqueous  amino  acid  salts  were  very  good absorbents  for  MGA  and  compatible  with  polypropylene membrane [6] . For CO2 removal with membrane-based amino acid  salts,  Kumar  et  al  further  found  that  amino  acid  salts possess  stable  physical  natures  and  are  resistant to the wetting  of  the  micropores  of  polypropylene  membrane [7] . However,  the  aqueous  amino  acid  salts  are  difficult  for regeneration. The performance of these absorbents can evidently be improved by adding various activating agents [8] . Therefore, it is essential to search for an activating agent so that  the  activated  absorbent  (AAb)  of  amino  acid  salt  can possess excellent performances of both absorption and regeneration.
texts.text: purification  in  80-90s  of  the  last  century.  Moreover,  the aqueous  amino  acid  salts  are  also  used  for  the  selective absorption  of  H2S  from  H2S/CO2 [5] in  the  Alkacid  and Alkacid DIK processes of BASF and Exxon. Environment & Energy  Center,  TNO  the  Netherlands,  developed  a  process called CORAL, which applied the aqueous amino acid salts to  MGA.  The  aqueous  amino  acid  salts  were  very  good absorbents  for  MGA  and  compatible  with  polypropylene membrane [6] . For CO2 removal with membrane-based amino acid  salts,  Kumar  et  al  further  found  that  amino  acid  salts possess  stable  physical  natures  and  are  resistant to the wetting  of  the  micropores  of  polypropylene  membrane [7] . However,  the  aqueous  amino  acid  salts  are  difficult  for regeneration. The performance of these absorbents can evidently be improved by adding various activating agents [8] . Therefore, it is essential to search for an activating agent so that  the  activated  absorbent  (AAb)  of  amino  acid  salt  can possess excellent performances of both absorption and regeneration.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1    Reaction and activation mechanism
texts.text: 1    Reaction and activation mechanism

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Eq. 1, amino acid(I) is dissolved into water and hydrolyzed into a dipole ion(II). Successively, the dipole ion(II) is dually ionized to form a protonated cation(III) and a  deprotonated anion (IV), as shown in Eq. 2. At this time, the pH value of the solution is equal to the isoelectric point of  the  amino  acid  (for  GLY, pH = 5.97). Multispecies ions and multiequilibrium exist in the solution. Among these ions, only  the  deprotonated  amine  species  (IV)  can  lively  react with the acid gases.
texts.text: As shown in Eq. 1, amino acid(I) is dissolved into water and hydrolyzed into a dipole ion(II). Successively, the dipole ion(II) is dually ionized to form a protonated cation(III) and a  deprotonated anion (IV), as shown in Eq. 2. At this time, the pH value of the solution is equal to the isoelectric point of  the  amino  acid  (for  GLY, pH = 5.97). Multispecies ions and multiequilibrium exist in the solution. Among these ions, only  the  deprotonated  amine  species  (IV)  can  lively  react with the acid gases.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H2 O HOOC-R-NH2 (I) - OOC-R-NH3 + (II)                        (1)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: -H + HOOC-R-NH3 + (III) - OOC-R-NH3 + (II) -OOC-R-NH2 (IV) -H + (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  reaction  of  amino  acid  salts  with  CO 2 may  follow  the zwitterionic mechanism [7] :
texts.text: The  reaction  of  amino  acid  salts  with  CO 2 may  follow  the zwitterionic mechanism [7] :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 2 OOC-R-NH 2   +   C O 2 -OOC-R-NHCOO -+ -OOC-R-NH 3 + (3)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: - OOC-R-NH3 + - OOC-R-NH2  +  H + (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When  phosphates  as  activating  agents  are  added  in  the aqueous  amino  acid  salts,  following  multilevel  equilibrium reactions occur in the solution (Eq. 5). The activating agent phosphates can then promote the reaction of amino acid salts with CO2 and then accelerate the mass transfer of CO2.
texts.text: When  phosphates  as  activating  agents  are  added  in  the aqueous  amino  acid  salts,  following  multilevel  equilibrium reactions occur in the solution (Eq. 5). The activating agent phosphates can then promote the reaction of amino acid salts with CO2 and then accelerate the mass transfer of CO2.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PO4 3+ H 2 O HPO4 2+ OH  H2O H2PO 4 -+ OH  -+ .          (5)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  multilevel  hydrolyzation  (Eq.  5)  of  phosphates  can moderate the pH value of the solution to avoid the decrease of  pH  value  as  CO2  enters  the  solution.  Therefore,  the phosphates can catalyze the reaction of the amino acid salts with CO2 through Eq. 5 and sustain the activity of the amino acid salts.
texts.text: The  multilevel  hydrolyzation  (Eq.  5)  of  phosphates  can moderate the pH value of the solution to avoid the decrease of  pH  value  as  CO2  enters  the  solution.  Therefore,  the phosphates can catalyze the reaction of the amino acid salts with CO2 through Eq. 5 and sustain the activity of the amino acid salts.

texts.label: text

Ltd., China), K3PO4, K2HPO4, and KH2PO4  (China  Medicament  Group  Shanghai  Chemical Reagent  Co.)  were  used.  Aqueous  amino  acid  salts  were prepared with deionized water and  neutralized with an equimolar  quantity  of  potassium  hydroxide.  N2  and  CO2 were commercial cylinder gases with the purity higher than

Ltd., China), K3PO4, K2HPO4, and KH2PO4  (China  Medicament  Group  Shanghai  Chemical Reagent  Co.)  were  used.  Aqueous  amino  acid  salts  were prepared with deionized water and  neutralized with an equimolar  quantity  of  potassium  hydroxide.  N2  and  CO2 were commercial cylinder gases with the purity higher than

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 99.99% (Nanjing Real Special-gas Co., China). The properties of hydrophobic polypropylene hollow fiber membrane  module  were  as follows: inner diameter of module, 32 mm; outer diameter of fiber membrane, 500 P m; available length of fiber membrane, 250 mm; wall thickness of  fiber  membrane,  100 P m;  membrane micropore, 0.1-0.5 P m; porosity, 0.50-0.60; and number of fibers, 2000.
texts.text: 99.99% (Nanjing Real Special-gas Co., China). The properties of hydrophobic polypropylene hollow fiber membrane  module  were  as follows: inner diameter of module, 32 mm; outer diameter of fiber membrane, 500 P m; available length of fiber membrane, 250 mm; wall thickness of  fiber  membrane,  100 P m;  membrane micropore, 0.1-0.5 P m; porosity, 0.50-0.60; and number of fibers, 2000.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2    Setup and procedures
texts.text: 2.2    Setup and procedures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 is a schematic of the experimental setup for CO2 capture. Before  the tests,  the  gas  mixture  N2/CO2  was prepared  with  a  given  CO2  concentration.  The  mixed-gas stream from the gas cylinder (1) is passed through a reducing valve  and  a  gas  flow  meter  (2),  and  then  was  fed  into  the fiber  lumen  at  the  top  side  of  the  module  (3).  The  liquid absorbent  was  introduced  by  a  gear  pump  (6)  from  the solution tank (8) and through a liquid-flow meter (7), to the shell side of the module in a counterflow mode. CO2 in the gas mixture in the fiber lumen diffused through the membrane pores into the liquid in the shell side and was then absorbed by the solution. The gas stream after  CO2 absorption was released from bottom side of the module. The solution which contained CO2 entered a stripper (4) and was hot-regenerated.  The  fresh  solution  would  be  cycled  to  the liquid  inlet  of  the  membrane  contactor.  The  vapor  stream containing  CO2  was  exhausted  out  of  the  stripper  top.  The vapor stream was cooled down through a water-cooler (5). In the  gas-liquid  separator  (9),  CO 2 was  separated  from  the condensate water that was returned to the top of the stripper. The  fresh  solution  which  had  been  regenerated  was  cooled through  the  water-cooler  (11)  and  returned  to  the  solution tank  (8).  The  solution  was  used  circularly  in  this  system. Zero-loading of the absorbent could ensure high drive force for the CO2 transfer.
texts.text: Figure 1 is a schematic of the experimental setup for CO2 capture. Before  the tests,  the  gas  mixture  N2/CO2  was prepared  with  a  given  CO2  concentration.  The  mixed-gas stream from the gas cylinder (1) is passed through a reducing valve  and  a  gas  flow  meter  (2),  and  then  was  fed  into  the fiber  lumen  at  the  top  side  of  the  module  (3).  The  liquid absorbent  was  introduced  by  a  gear  pump  (6)  from  the solution tank (8) and through a liquid-flow meter (7), to the shell side of the module in a counterflow mode. CO2 in the gas mixture in the fiber lumen diffused through the membrane pores into the liquid in the shell side and was then absorbed by the solution. The gas stream after  CO2 absorption was released from bottom side of the module. The solution which contained CO2 entered a stripper (4) and was hot-regenerated.  The  fresh  solution  would  be  cycled  to  the liquid  inlet  of  the  membrane  contactor.  The  vapor  stream containing  CO2  was  exhausted  out  of  the  stripper  top.  The vapor stream was cooled down through a water-cooler (5). In the  gas-liquid  separator  (9),  CO 2 was  separated  from  the condensate water that was returned to the top of the stripper. The  fresh  solution  which  had  been  regenerated  was  cooled through  the  water-cooler  (11)  and  returned  to  the  solution tank  (8).  The  solution  was  used  circularly  in  this  system. Zero-loading of the absorbent could ensure high drive force for the CO2 transfer.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3    Analysis methods and data processing
texts.text: 2.3    Analysis methods and data processing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The content of CO2 in the gas phase was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC6890; Agilent Co., USA). The components in the liquid phase were analyzed by another gas chromatography  (CP3380;  Varian  Co.,  USA).  The  total amine  concentration  was  determined  by  titration  with  a standard acid solution using methyl orange as indicator. The CO2 content in the liquid phase was determined by chemical analysis.
texts.text: The content of CO2 in the gas phase was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC6890; Agilent Co., USA). The components in the liquid phase were analyzed by another gas chromatography  (CP3380;  Varian  Co.,  USA).  The  total amine  concentration  was  determined  by  titration  with  a standard acid solution using methyl orange as indicator. The CO2 content in the liquid phase was determined by chemical analysis.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3    Results and discussion
texts.text: 3    Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1    Comparison of activating performance of phosphates
texts.text: 3.1    Comparison of activating performance of phosphates

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Phosphoric acid is a polyprotonic inorganic acid, corresponding salts are PO4 3,  HPO4 2, and  H2PO4 -.  Various phosphates in water are different in the hydrolytic degree and ionization. Potassium phosphates were added into the aqueous  amino  acid salt as the activating agents.  The performance  of  phosphate-AAb  of  amino  acid  salt  for  CO 2 capture  was  then  studied  under  22 o C,  gas  flow  rate  ( G )  of 1.11 u 10 -5 m 3 /s,  overall  absorbent  concentration  of  0.5 mol/L,  activating  agent  concentration  of  0.1  mol/L  in  the solution,  CO2  mole  percent  of  10.16%  in  gas  phase,  and certain liquid flow rate ( V L ).
texts.text: Phosphoric acid is a polyprotonic inorganic acid, corresponding salts are PO4 3,  HPO4 2, and  H2PO4 -.  Various phosphates in water are different in the hydrolytic degree and ionization. Potassium phosphates were added into the aqueous  amino  acid salt as the activating agents.  The performance  of  phosphate-AAb  of  amino  acid  salt  for  CO 2 capture  was  then  studied  under  22 o C,  gas  flow  rate  ( G )  of 1.11 u 10 -5 m 3 /s,  overall  absorbent  concentration  of  0.5 mol/L,  activating  agent  concentration  of  0.1  mol/L  in  the solution,  CO2  mole  percent  of  10.16%  in  gas  phase,  and certain liquid flow rate ( V L ).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2    Activation performances of the multivalent phosphates on the absorbent of amino acid salts for CO2 capture : 0.5 mol/L GLY; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L KH2PO4; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L K2HPO4; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L K3PO4
texts.text: Fig. 2    Activation performances of the multivalent phosphates on the absorbent of amino acid salts for CO2 capture : 0.5 mol/L GLY; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L KH2PO4; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L K2HPO4; : 0.4 mol/L GLY+0.1 mol/L K3PO4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 2, with the increase of liquid flow rate, the overall mass transfer coefficients K G D of various activating absorbents increase. The increase extent of K G D decreases  with  the  increase  of  liquid  flow  rate.  The values of K G D for  three  phosphate-activating absorbents are larger than that of single amino acid salt, indicating that by adding activating agent, the performance of a single absorbent can be improved and the mass transfer of membrane contactor can be enhanced. Among these phosphates, the activated effect of K3PO4  is the most significant; their activated effects are in the order of PO 4 3-> HPO4 2>  H2PO4 -.  These  can  be  explained  by  the  catalytic action of  multilevel equilibria  of  phosphates in the solution on  the  reaction  of  amino  acid  salt  with  CO2.  OH -created from  Eq.  5  reacts  with  H + from  Eq.  4  to  form  H2O; consequently, Eq. 4 moves to the right. Zwitterion -OOCRNH3 +  is farther reverted to the active ion  - OOCRNH2 (i.e. the anion IV in Eq. 2). In this way, the mass transfer is enhanced, and the value of K G D increases. The hydrolyzation of PO4 3and HPO4 2is much easier than that of H2PO4 -, as K b of PO4 3and HPO4 2is far higher than that of H2PO4 -; the ion concentration  of  OH -produced  from  PO4 3and  HPO4 2is then far higher than that from H2PO4 -.
texts.text: As shown in Fig. 2, with the increase of liquid flow rate, the overall mass transfer coefficients K G D of various activating absorbents increase. The increase extent of K G D decreases  with  the  increase  of  liquid  flow  rate.  The values of K G D for  three  phosphate-activating absorbents are larger than that of single amino acid salt, indicating that by adding activating agent, the performance of a single absorbent can be improved and the mass transfer of membrane contactor can be enhanced. Among these phosphates, the activated effect of K3PO4  is the most significant; their activated effects are in the order of PO 4 3-> HPO4 2>  H2PO4 -.  These  can  be  explained  by  the  catalytic action of  multilevel equilibria  of  phosphates in the solution on  the  reaction  of  amino  acid  salt  with  CO2.  OH -created from  Eq.  5  reacts  with  H + from  Eq.  4  to  form  H2O; consequently, Eq. 4 moves to the right. Zwitterion -OOCRNH3 +  is farther reverted to the active ion  - OOCRNH2 (i.e. the anion IV in Eq. 2). In this way, the mass transfer is enhanced, and the value of K G D increases. The hydrolyzation of PO4 3and HPO4 2is much easier than that of H2PO4 -, as K b of PO4 3and HPO4 2is far higher than that of H2PO4 -; the ion concentration  of  OH -produced  from  PO4 3and  HPO4 2is then far higher than that from H2PO4 -.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2    Effect of the activating agent concentration on the mass transfer flux
texts.text: 3.2    Effect of the activating agent concentration on the mass transfer flux

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of the activating agent K3PO4 concentration on the mass transfer flux was determined at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 3, under the constant overall concentration of 0.5 mol/L, the concentration of K3PO4 in the solution can be  obtained  by  altering  the  proportion  of  K3PO4  and  GLY (i.e. the concentrations of K3PO4 + GLY in mol/L are 0 + 0.5, 0.1 + 0.4, 0.2 + 0.3, and 0.3 + 0.2, and so on).
texts.text: The effect of the activating agent K3PO4 concentration on the mass transfer flux was determined at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 3, under the constant overall concentration of 0.5 mol/L, the concentration of K3PO4 in the solution can be  obtained  by  altering  the  proportion  of  K3PO4  and  GLY (i.e. the concentrations of K3PO4 + GLY in mol/L are 0 + 0.5, 0.1 + 0.4, 0.2 + 0.3, and 0.3 + 0.2, and so on).

texts.orig: Other  operation  conditions  were  as  follows:  21 o C,  gas flow rate ( G ) of 1.11 × 10 -5 m 3 /s, liquid flow rate ( V L ) of 100 mL/min,  CO2  content  in  gas  phase  ( y CO2)  of  10.81%.  The mass  transfer  flux  increases  with  the  increase  of  K3PO4 concentration, reaches a maximum at a K3PO4 concentration of 0.2 mol/L, and then decrease with the further increase of K3PO4.  As  the  concentration  of  K3PO4  in  the  proportion increases, namely the concentration of GLY in the proportion decreases,  the  concentration  of  hydroxyl  ion  created  from phosphate hydrolyzation increases. Then, following reaction takes place as follows:
texts.text: Other  operation  conditions  were  as  follows:  21 o C,  gas flow rate ( G ) of 1.11 × 10 -5 m 3 /s, liquid flow rate ( V L ) of 100 mL/min,  CO2  content  in  gas  phase  ( y CO2)  of  10.81%.  The mass  transfer  flux  increases  with  the  increase  of  K3PO4 concentration, reaches a maximum at a K3PO4 concentration of 0.2 mol/L, and then decrease with the further increase of K3PO4.  As  the  concentration  of  K3PO4  in  the  proportion increases, namely the concentration of GLY in the proportion decreases,  the  concentration  of  hydroxyl  ion  created  from phosphate hydrolyzation increases. Then, following reaction takes place as follows:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: CO2  +  OH -HCO3 -(9)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, its velocity is far less than that of  the  reaction given by Eq. 3 [9] . As a result, the mass transfer flux decreases at K3PO4 concentration of higher than 0.2 mol/L. This shows that  a  certain  amount  of  activating  agent  is  sufficient  to promote the CO2 capture; the activation effect is essentially identical to the phase catalysis.
texts.text: However, its velocity is far less than that of  the  reaction given by Eq. 3 [9] . As a result, the mass transfer flux decreases at K3PO4 concentration of higher than 0.2 mol/L. This shows that  a  certain  amount  of  activating  agent  is  sufficient  to promote the CO2 capture; the activation effect is essentially identical to the phase catalysis.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3    Effect of AAb on capture efficiency
texts.text: 3.3    Effect of AAb on capture efficiency

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4    Effect of activated absorbent on the efficiency of CO2 capture
texts.text: Fig. 4    Effect of activated absorbent on the efficiency of CO2 capture

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: : 0.5 mol/L GLY; : 0.3 mol/L GLY + 0.2 mol/L K3PO4
texts.text: : 0.5 mol/L GLY; : 0.3 mol/L GLY + 0.2 mol/L K3PO4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  capture  efficiency  is  an  important  index  for  the industrial  application  of  MGA.  Various  tests  were  then performed to determine the capture efficiencies of the AAb and non-AAb, as shown in Fig. 4 (19 o C, gas flow rate ( G ) of 1.11 × 10 -5 m 3 /s, CO2 content in gas phase ( y CO2) of 10.55%). CO2  capture  efficiency  increases  with  the  increase  of  the absorbent flow rate; however, the extent of capture efficiency increase  is  not  so  distinct  when  the  flow  rate  reaches  a certain  value  (125  mL/min).  Additionally,  Fig.  4  illustrates strongly  that  the  capture  efficiency  of  the  non-AAb  is  far lower than that of the AAb; the maximal capture efficiency of AAb can reach 90% while it is only 70% for the non-AAb GLY.
texts.text: The  capture  efficiency  is  an  important  index  for  the industrial  application  of  MGA.  Various  tests  were  then performed to determine the capture efficiencies of the AAb and non-AAb, as shown in Fig. 4 (19 o C, gas flow rate ( G ) of 1.11 × 10 -5 m 3 /s, CO2 content in gas phase ( y CO2) of 10.55%). CO2  capture  efficiency  increases  with  the  increase  of  the absorbent flow rate; however, the extent of capture efficiency increase  is  not  so  distinct  when  the  flow  rate  reaches  a certain  value  (125  mL/min).  Additionally,  Fig.  4  illustrates strongly  that  the  capture  efficiency  of  the  non-AAb  is  far lower than that of the AAb; the maximal capture efficiency of AAb can reach 90% while it is only 70% for the non-AAb GLY.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4    Effect of operation factors on the mass transfer
texts.text: 3.4    Effect of operation factors on the mass transfer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effects of operation factors, such as the gas and liquid flow rates, on the mass transfer in MGA  were  also determined under 19 o C,  CO2  content  in  gas  phase  ( y CO2)  of 10.06%, AAb concentration of 0.3 mol/L GLY + 0.2 mol/L K3PO4, as shown in Fig. 5. The mass transfer flux increases with the increase of both the gas and liquid flow rates. The fluid state in the membrane contactor may change along with the increase of gas and liquid flow rates.  Re  number increases  with  increasing  the  gas  and  liquid  flow  rates;  the turbulence  degree  trends  to  enlarge  and  the  thicknesses  of gas  and  liquid  phase's  boundary  layers  in  the  gas-liquid interface trend to diminish. The mass transfer resistance then decreases and the mass transfer flux increases accordingly.
texts.text: The effects of operation factors, such as the gas and liquid flow rates, on the mass transfer in MGA  were  also determined under 19 o C,  CO2  content  in  gas  phase  ( y CO2)  of 10.06%, AAb concentration of 0.3 mol/L GLY + 0.2 mol/L K3PO4, as shown in Fig. 5. The mass transfer flux increases with the increase of both the gas and liquid flow rates. The fluid state in the membrane contactor may change along with the increase of gas and liquid flow rates.  Re  number increases  with  increasing  the  gas  and  liquid  flow  rates;  the turbulence  degree  trends  to  enlarge  and  the  thicknesses  of gas  and  liquid  phase's  boundary  layers  in  the  gas-liquid interface trend to diminish. The mass transfer resistance then decreases and the mass transfer flux increases accordingly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Meanwhile, the mass transfer flux levels off, when the gas and liquid flow rates reach a certain value; an optimum value of  gas  and  liquid  follow  rates  is  present,  which  is  able  to attain  a  maximum  mass  transfer  flux  of  the  membrane contactor and minimum power consumption.
texts.text: Meanwhile, the mass transfer flux levels off, when the gas and liquid flow rates reach a certain value; an optimum value of  gas  and  liquid  follow  rates  is  present,  which  is  able  to attain  a  maximum  mass  transfer  flux  of  the  membrane contactor and minimum power consumption.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5    Effect of operation factors on the mass transfer flux : G =1.11×10 -5 m 3 /s; : G =1.67×10 -5 m 3 /s
texts.text: Fig. 5    Effect of operation factors on the mass transfer flux : G =1.11×10 -5 m 3 /s; : G =1.67×10 -5 m 3 /s

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: LU Jian-gang et al. / Journal of Fuel Chemistry and Technology, 2009, 37(1): 77 81
texts.text: LU Jian-gang et al. / Journal of Fuel Chemistry and Technology, 2009, 37(1): 77 81

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the gas and liquid phases are isolated by the membrane, the gas and liquid flow rates are independent on each other, the  operation  condition  can be  achieved  upon  practical application. These suggest that the changes in hydrodynamics  of  MGA  can  improve  the  mass  transfer performance  of  membrane  contactor  and  enhance  the  mass transfer efficiency to a certain extent. The essential factor for enhancing  the  mass  transfer  efficiency  in  MGA  is  the performance of the absorbents used.
texts.text: As the gas and liquid phases are isolated by the membrane, the gas and liquid flow rates are independent on each other, the  operation  condition  can be  achieved  upon  practical application. These suggest that the changes in hydrodynamics  of  MGA  can  improve  the  mass  transfer performance  of  membrane  contactor  and  enhance  the  mass transfer efficiency to a certain extent. The essential factor for enhancing  the  mass  transfer  efficiency  in  MGA  is  the performance of the absorbents used.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4    Conclusions
texts.text: 4    Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyprotonic  phosphates  PO4 3,  HPO4 2,  and  H2PO4 -as activating agents in aqueous amino acid salt can improve the capacity of CO2 capture in MGA. The activation extents of the phosphates were in the order of PO4 3> HPO4 2> H2PO4 -.
texts.text: Polyprotonic  phosphates  PO4 3,  HPO4 2,  and  H2PO4 -as activating agents in aqueous amino acid salt can improve the capacity of CO2 capture in MGA. The activation extents of the phosphates were in the order of PO4 3> HPO4 2> H2PO4 -.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the increase of the activating agent concentration in the absorbent, the mass transfer flux and the activation effect increases at first, reaches a maximum at certain concentration, and then decrease with the further increase of the activating agent concentration. A certain amount of activating agent is sufficient to promote the CO2 capture; the activation effect is essentially identical to the phase catalysis.
texts.text: With the increase of the activating agent concentration in the absorbent, the mass transfer flux and the activation effect increases at first, reaches a maximum at certain concentration, and then decrease with the further increase of the activating agent concentration. A certain amount of activating agent is sufficient to promote the CO2 capture; the activation effect is essentially identical to the phase catalysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Under  the  same  operation  conditions,  the  efficiency  of CO2 capture with AAb is far higher than that with non-AAb. The  performance  of  activated  amino  acid  salt  absorbent  is obviously superior to that of single amino acid salt.
texts.text: Under  the  same  operation  conditions,  the  efficiency  of CO2 capture with AAb is far higher than that with non-AAb. The  performance  of  activated  amino  acid  salt  absorbent  is obviously superior to that of single amino acid salt.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The changes in the hydrodynamics of MGA can improve the  mass  transfer  performance  of  membrane  contactor  and enhance the mass transfer efficiency to a certain extent. The essential factor for enhancing the mass transfer efficiency in MGA is the performance of the absorbents used.
texts.text: The changes in the hydrodynamics of MGA can improve the  mass  transfer  performance  of  membrane  contactor  and enhance the mass transfer efficiency to a certain extent. The essential factor for enhancing the mass transfer efficiency in MGA is the performance of the absorbents used.
